coredata.dc:title: A design of fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of dopamine via FRET as well as live cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Kumari, Archana
Mukherjee, Poulami
Saikia, Tinku
Pal, Kunal
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine is an essential biomolecule and transmits neurological signals in humans and other animals. Either deficiency or excessive concentration of dopamine cause several diseases in human beings like Huntington’s disease, fatal genetic disorder, and Parkinson’s disease. This study demonstrates the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) from porcine pancreatic lipase (PPL) via the pyrolysis method. These synthesized CDs are used as a fluorescence probe for the selective detection of dopamine with a limit of detection 20 nM. This selectivity is due to the tendency of dopamine to oxidize into dopamine-quinone, which adsorbed on the surface of CDs. The emission intensity of CDs can be effectively quenched by dopamine-quinone via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. The sensing mechanism was characterized by the fluorescence lifetime decay, zeta potential, and UV–visible spectroscopy analysis. More importantly, the synthesized CDs are also used for the naked-eye detection of dopamine and real samples analysis. Additionally, CDs showed low-cytotoxicity toward MDA-MB 468 cells, demonstrating their essential use for in vitro dopamine sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine sensing
Fluorescent CDs
FRET process
Porcine pancreatic lipase
In vitro cell imaging
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Particles size and surface morphology analysis
Detection of dopamine by the fluorometric method
In vitro cell imaging and cytotoxicity study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad , India.
This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths (330–400 nm) and (b) the UV–visible absorption of CDs, dopamine, and after the addition of dopamine into CDs [inset figure shows that the absorption spectra of the oxidized form of dopamine (dopamine-quinone)].
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) SAED pattern of the synthesized CDs.
(a) TEM image [inset particles size distribution diagram], (b) HRTEM image [Inset DLS analysis of CDs], (c) FESEM image, and (d) EDX spectra [Inset elemental composition] of the synthesized CDs.
(a) Full range XPS spectrum and (b, c, d, and e) high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p peaks of CDs, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of the CDs with increasing the concentration of dopamine from 0.01 to 100 µM and (b) linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of dopamine [Inset Stern-Volmer graph with the concentration range of 1–50 µM].

(a) interfering biomolecules of 100 µM citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Sur), norepinephrine (Norepi), and epinephrine (Epi) for dopamine (DA) detection and (b) visual detection of dopamine on TLC paper as well as in aqueous medium with different concentration [0–100 µM] of dopamine.

(a) Spectral overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of dopamine-quinone during the FRET process, (b) fluorescent lifetime decay, and (c) & (d) zeta potential analysis of CDs in the presence and absence of dopamine, respectively.
(a) FRET ratio plot with respect to the concentrations of dopamine and (b) with respect to reaction time.

Fluorometric detection of dopamine in [(a) human blood serum, (b) urine samples] by the synthesized CDs, and (c) the cytotoxicity study of the synthesized CDs at difference concentrations [0–100 µg/mL].
NCFM image of MDA-MB 468 cells incubated with the 10 µg/mL of CDs and after dopamine solution at excitation wavelength [450–480 nm] and merge field.
Graphical representation for the preparation of CDs from PPL and used to detect dopamine and in vitro cellular imaging.

(a) Schematic representation of the FRET-based sensing platform of dopamine sensing as well as (b) diagrammatic representation of (1) electron transfer process and (2) charge transfer process from an excited state CDs to the ground state of dopamine-quinone.
The comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods of dopamine determination.
Analytical results for measurement of dopamine in human blood serum as well as urine samples.

head.ce:title: A design of fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of dopamine via FRET as well as live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, S.B. College (V.K.S.U), Ara 802301, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Petroleum Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad 826004, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bangal 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, West Bangal 700032, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine is an essential biomolecule and transmits neurological signals in humans and other animals. Either deficiency or excessive concentration of dopamine cause several diseases in human beings like Huntington’s disease, fatal genetic disorder, and Parkinson’s disease. This study demonstrates the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) from porcine pancreatic lipase (PPL) via the pyrolysis method. These synthesized CDs are used as a fluorescence probe for the selective detection of dopamine with a limit of detection 20 nM. This selectivity is due to the tendency of dopamine to oxidize into dopamine-quinone, which adsorbed on the surface of CDs. The emission intensity of CDs can be effectively quenched by dopamine-quinone via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. The sensing mechanism was characterized by the fluorescence lifetime decay, zeta potential, and UV–visible spectroscopy analysis. More importantly, the synthesized CDs are also used for the naked-eye detection of dopamine and real samples analysis. Additionally, CDs showed low-cytotoxicity toward MDA-MB 468 cells, demonstrating their essential use for in vitro dopamine sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine is the essential neurotransmitters released by neurons and mainly exists in the brain and the central nervous system (CNS) in humans and other animals [1–4]. It plays a crucial role, including movement, prolactin production inhibition, memory, learning, and transmits neurological signals [1–6]. The endogenous concentration of dopamine in the human brain and bovine serum is 1 × 10−9–10−5 mol/L [4]. Additionally, the excessive discharge of dopamine causes many diseases in the human body, including Huntington’s disease and genetic disorder, and its deficiency causes Parkinson’s disease [1–5]. Thus, the initial detection of dopamine in the human body is crucial for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research [7]. Right now, dopamine detection from complex matrices is still a challenging issue. Therefore, it is important to improve the selectivity and sensitivity towards dopamine detection.

Recently, numerous materials and procedures have been established for the sensing of dopamine [8–10]. For example, Zhao et al. developed a 3D-carbon nanotube electrode for dopamine sensing by cyclic voltammetry process [1]. Zhang et al. developed a boronic acid-functionalized pyrene probe for dopamine sensing by the electrochemiluminescence method [8]. Fabre et al. synthesized boronic acid-functionalized oxide-free silicon surfaces for dopamine sensing by the electrochemical process [9]. Among all these reported methods, the electrochemical method has attracted influential attention in recent years due to low cost and high sensitivity [10]. However, some other biomolecules (uric acid & ascorbic acid) whose oxidation potential is near dopamine and their concentration levels in human blood serum are 100–1000 times higher than dopamine [3,11]. So, overlap of the voltammetric response of these compounds in the electrochemical method makes their selective and sensitive detection of dopamine is complicated [11]. Nowadays, the fluorometric method overcomes these problems due to its high sensitivity, good reproducibility, cheap, and simplified optical setup [12–15]. Recently, many researchers have paid more attention to detect dopamine via the fluorometric method [11–14]. For example, Qian et al. synthesized phenylboronic acid functionalized polymer nanoparticles to detect dopamine [4]. Weng et al. fabricated poly-dopamine decorated graphene quantum dots by fluorescence turn-off sensing of dopamine [2]. Zhang et al. synthesized silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) for the selective detection of dopamine [13]. Chen et al. developed GO-based fluorescent nanomaterials for the detection of dopamine [12]. However, all these above reported fluorescent materials are developed by the tedious synthesis processes, like doping and composite forming with other nanoparticles as well as polymers [4,12–14]. So, it highly needs to develop fluorescent material via a simple synthetic method to detect dopamine. Over the last decade, CDs have attracted huge attention due to their outstanding photophysical properties [16–19]. Earlier, few researchers have been synthesized CDs for dopamine sensing via the fluorometric method, such as Yildirim et al. prepared polydopamine nanoparticles for dopamine sensing [20]. Zhao et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots for dopamine detection [11]. The articles mentioned above reported the direct sensing of dopamine using fluorescent carbon dots. For further investigation, new fluorescent materials with alternate methods are needful for the detection of dopamine.

We first-time report CDs synthesized from porcine pancreatic lipase (PPL) via pyrolysis method with a high quantum yield (21%). PPL is one of the most widely used lipases in biotransformation reactions because it is cost-effective compared to the other commercial lipases [21]. It is composed of several amino acids such as aspartate, histidine, glutamate, and serine [21]. The aqueous solution of synthesized CDs is slightly alkaline due to the presence of amino acids. This alkaline solution of CDs can easily oxidize the dopamine into dopamine-quinone in the presence of atmospheric oxygen. This oxidized form dopamine-quinone can adsorb on the surface of CDs by electrostatic interaction and intermolecular H-bonding [12–14]. These H-bonding are formed between the cis-diol of dopamine and amino & carboxylic groups present on the surface of CDs. These synthesized CDs are highly sensitive and selective towards dopamine with a limit of detection (20 nM) with an extensive linear range of 1–50 μM. Furthermore, CDs can be utilized in the visual detection of dopamine both on TLC paper and aqueous medium. Additionally, CDs are also used for the detection of dopamine in real samples and in-vitro cellular imaging.

In recent years, few fluorescent-based mechanisms have been used for the detection of dopamine, including förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced charge transfer (PET), charge transfer (CT), and inner filter effect (IFE) [22–29]. Among the several photophysical phenomena explored for fluorescence-based sensing, FRET is a very effective method and readily used for sensing applications [24–28]. The FRET process occurs between the excited state donors to the optically active acceptor having appropriate energy levels from visible to near-infrared region via nonradiative energy transfer within the Förster distance of <50 Å [12–14]. These conditions can favor the FRET process. Till now, few groups have been reported FRET-based detection of dopamine [2,29]. In this work, the emission intensity of CDs can be quenched by dopamine, probably due to the FRET mechanism. This sensing mechanism was supported by the UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime decay analysis.

Here, CDs were synthesized by a simple pyrolysis method [15–19]. In brief, PPL (30 mg) was taken in a silica crucible and heated at 250 °C for 1 h in a furnace with air. The obtained black product was collected and dissolved into water (40 mL). After that, the dissolved solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 8 min to eliminate unwanted nanoparticles. Finally, CDs solution was dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag in distilled water for 24 h and the obtained product was stored at below 4 °C for further applications. The synthesized CDs solution shows the bright-blue fluorescent color under UV-light (365 nm). After dopamine addition, the bright-bluish fluorescent color of CDs is quenched and is shown in Scheme 1
.

UV–visible spectra were measured on a Perkin-Elmer 950 spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements were carried out using fluorescence spectrometer Perkin Elmer LS 55. FT-IR spectra were recorded on Agilent carry 660 instruments. X-ray diffraction was analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (Phillips PW 1710 with Cu-Kα radiation). The TEM image was examined by HR-TEM (HITACHI H-8100). Morphology and elemental composition were analyzed by FESEM (Supra 55). Zeta potential and DLS study were performed on Nano ZS (Malvern). The multicolor cell imaging of GL261 cells was performed using a fluorescence microscope (Hitachi 7400). MTT assay was determined using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader at 490 nm (BioTek, USA). The fluorescence lifetime decay was performed on Fluoro Max-4 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). To determine the bonding configurations and elemental compositions of the synthesized CDs by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.

To determine the potential application of CDs was used for cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 cells. In brief, MDA-MB 468 cells (2 mL) cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with penicillin (100 µg/mL), 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), and streptomycin (100 µg/mL) at 37 °C in a humidified under 5% CO2 for 24 h [15–18]. For in vitro cell imaging studied, MDA-MB 468 cells (106 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The cells were then treated with 10 µM of the CDs and (CDs + dopamine) solution and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C and then washed with PBS three times to remove the extra materials [11,21]. Finally, the samples were examined using a glass slide and observed under a fluorescence microscope (Leica). The fluorescent image of MDA-MB 468 cells was completely diminished after the addition of dopamine solution.

In vitro cytotoxicity of the CDs was examined by MTT assay. Briefly, 100 µL of MDA-MB 468 cells were seeded in 96-well microplate with a density of 106 cells per well and allowed to adhere overnight. After incubation of 24 h at 37 °C, the culture medium was removed, and cells were treated with various concentrations of CDs (0–100 µg/ml) for another 24 h. After incubation, the culture medium was discarded, and a fresh culture medium containing 50 µL of MTT (10 mg/ml) was added into each well and incubated for the next 4 h. After incubation of 4 h, the growth medium was removed, and 200 µL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolved MTT. Finally, the optical density was recorded of each sample by a microplate reader at 470 nm wavelength. The cell viability was calculated by the following formula [30].
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
T h e
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
e x p e r i m e n t a l
g r o u p
T h e
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p
× 100 %

The photoluminescence and UV–visible spectroscopy analysis examine the optical properties of the synthesized CDs. When the excitation wavelengths of CDs vary from 330 to 400 nm, the emission spectra of CDs are also changed with the peak shifting in higher wavelengths. These obtained results are consisting of the earlier reported articles [17–19]. It displays maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 340 and 445 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). It may be possible at this particular excitation wavelength (340 nm); many particles will be excited compared to the other excitation wavelengths. Simultaneously, the UV–visible spectroscopy analysis demonstrates that the CDs show an absorption peak at 335 nm. This peak arises due to the n-π* electronic transition of the C=O of the carboxylic group (–COOH) found on the CDs’ surface. Concurrently, the UV–visible spectrum of dopamine shows a sharp absorption band at 280 nm due to π -π* electronic transition of C=C of the aromatic ring of dopamine [11–13]. After that, the addition of dopamine into CDs solution, dopamine could oxidize into dopamine-quinone and exhibits a new broad absorption band at 460 nm [11–13]. The oxidized form of dopamine exhibits two absorption peaks in the visible region at 285 and 460 nm, as well as acts as a very good electron acceptor for CDs [2,4,12,13]. The absorption band of both CDs and dopamine is decreased as well as the bathochromic shift from 280 to 285 nm and 335 to 340 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(b). These shiftings are due to the extent in conjugation between –COOH and –NH2 groups of CDs with dopamine cis-diol. Thus, these results confirm that the CDs are successfully combined with dopamine.

Concurrently, we have analyzed the pH-effect on the emission spectra of the synthesized CDs, adding different buffer solutions (pH = 2.0–14.0), as shown in Figure S-3(a). The emission intensity of CDs is first increases in the range of pH2-pH7 and decreases with a further increase in pH value up to pH14. This observation indicates that the fluorescence property of CDs depends on pH value strongly. Simultaneously, CDs’ fluorescence stability is checked under high ionic strength condition using different concentrations of NaCl solution (0.3–1.5 mol/L). The slight change in emission intensities of CDs is shown in Figure S-3(b). These results show that the synthesized CDs have high potential applications for sensors under sophisticated and drastic environmental conditions. Concurrently, CDs prepare at different temperatures (150–300 °C) and time (30–75 min), the maximum emission intensity was observed at 250 °C and 60 mins, respectively. This may be due to maximum carbonization of the PPL is possible at this particular temperature and time, as shown in Figure S-4(a) & (b). Parallel, the effects of different solvents (acetone, methanol, DMSO, DMF, water, DCM, THF, chloroform, ethanol, and toluene) on the synthesized CDs are also analyzed. The results demonstrate that the maximum emission intensity of CDs is observed in the water medium, as shown in Figure S-4(c). The fluorescence stability property of CDs to time, and there is no noticeable change in the emission spectra of CDs after the synthesis of two months, as shown in Figure S-4(d). We have also used the different types of lipases (PPL, amylase, CAL, and CRL) to prepare CDs and utilize them as a sensing probe for dopamine detection. The addition of dopamine solution into these CDs solution, there is no remarkable change in the emission spectra of CDs, except CDs synthesized from the PPL, as shown in Figure S-5[(a)-(d)]. So, CDs prepared from PPL are used as a sensing probe for the detection of dopamine.

The FTIR spectra of PPL show some characteristic peaks, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). A combined peak is observed at around 3300–3500 cm−1 due to N–H, S–H, and O–H stretching frequency of PPL. The peak originated at 2830 cm−1, corresponds to C–H stretching frequency. The absorption bands at 1630 cm−1, 1485 cm−1, 1340 cm−1, and 670 cm−1 indicate C=O, C=C, CH2 bending, and C-N stretching vibration modes, respectively. After the formation of CDs, some characteristic peaks of PPL disappeared in the FTIR spectrum analysis, as shown in Fig. 2(a).

The Raman spectrum analysis of the synthesized CDs is shown in Fig. 2(b). It shows a prominent peak at 1355 cm−1 (D-band), which arises from graphitic sp2 carbons. The peak at 1502 cm−1 (G-band) assigned due to the disordered structure of CDs. The relative ration of ID/IG is 1.32, which is closed to graphitic carbon. This result suggests that synthesized CDs are amorphous, as reported in previous literature [31–33].

XRD pattern clearly shows that the synthesized CDs possess an amorphous carbonaceous core and demonstrates two distinct peaks at 2θ = 31° & 55°, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(c) [17–19]. The peak observed at 2θ = 31° corresponds to the inter-planner distance of the order 0.22 nm, which confirms the diffraction pattern (002) of the amorphous carbon core of CDs. Simultaneously, the peak appears at 2θ = 55.3° corresponds to (101) planes, indicating that the synthesized CDs have a graphitic carbon core. The amorphous nature of CDs is also confirmed by the SAED pattern, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particles size and surface morphology analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size and surface morphology of the synthesized CDs were analyzed by TEM and FESEM analysis. The TEM image reveals that the CDs are spherical with size ranging between 1 and 5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), and it correlates with the narrow size distribution [inset of Fig. 3(a)]. HRTEM image shows the lattice structure of CDs with inter-fringes distance 0.22 nm is shown in Fig. 3(b). This demonstrates that the CDs are composed of graphitic carbon (002), which is correlated with the XRD and SAED pattern of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 3(c) & (d) [18]. Simultaneously, CDs hydrodynamic size is examined by the DLS analysis. The obtained result shows that the particle size of the CDs is about 220 nm due to the agglomeration of particles in the liquid media, as shown in [Inset of Fig. 3(b)]. Concurrently, FESEM analysis also confirms that the CDs are spherical, and a size ranging from 2 to 5 nm is shown in Fig. 3(c). From EDX analysis of the CDs, it is found that the CDs are composed of C (28.38%), N (8.88%), O (47.44%), Si (14.80%) and S (0.50%) elements, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The Si peak is due to the glass slide, which is used during the EDX analysis.

XPS analysis was used to identify the electronic states and compositions of the synthesized CDs. It shows that CDs are primarily composed of C, O, N, and S, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and S 2p of the CDs. The C 1 s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.4, 285.5, 286.5 eV, and 288.7 eV, which represent the C 1 s states in the C=C/C–H, C–C/C–S, H–O–C/C–N, and O–C=O/C=O bonds [34–37], respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The highly resolved N 1 s spectrum has been deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.6 and 400.6 eV corresponding to N–H and N–C=O species [38,39], respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The O 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks are centered at 531.4 eV, 532.6 eV, and 535.9 eV, which could be attributed to C=O, C–OH, and O=C–O–H bonds [40,41], respectively is shown in Fig. 4(d). The highly resolved S 2p spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 163.3 and 164.4 eV corresponding to C–S–C S 2p1/2 and C–S–C S 2p3/2 species [42,43], respectively is shown in Fig. 4(e). Thus, the XPS analysis confirms that the presence of six-carbon species (C=C/C–H, C–C/C–S, H–O–C/C–N, and O–C=O/C=O bonds) in the synthesized CDs. These results also correlate with the obtained FTIR bands, as shown in Fig. 2(a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dopamine by the fluorometric method
ce:section.ce:para: We have shown the feasibility of CDs for dopamine detection. It is observed that CDs possess a strong emission peak at 445 nm in the absence of dopamine. The quantum yield of CDs is 21% after that the addition of dopamine; it decreased to 2.3%. The fluorescence quenching spectra of CDs are analyzed by adding the different concentrations (0.01–100 µM) of dopamine, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The emission intensities of CDs are gradually decreased and the bathochromic shift in wavelengths (445 to 460 nm) with increasing the concentration of dopamine (0.01–100 µM) is observed, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The probable region behind this quenching, CDs solution can oxidize the dopamine into dopamine-quinone and adsorb on the surface of CDs by electrostatic interaction and intermolecular H-bonding [2,12–14]. The calibration curve is plotted between the emission intensities of CDs against dopamine concentrations, as shown in Fig. 5(b). It is observed that the quenching efficiency is directly proportional to the concentrations of dopamine. A good linear relationship [F0/F = 1.77 + 2.56 × C dopamine (100 µM)] was formed at the concentration range of 1–50 µM with correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.993), as shown in [inset Fig. 5(b)]. The limit of detection of dopamine is 20 nM based on 3 × SD/K. This result is analogous with previously reported methods for dopamine sensing, as shown in Table 1 .

Finally, we have checked the selectivity assay of CDs in the presence of possible interfering biomolecules such as citric acid, ascorbic acid (redox potential near dopamine), glucose, fructose, and sucrose into CDs solution. When interfering biomolecules are taken as equal concentration to dopamine (100 µM), there is no noticeable change observed in the emission intensity of CDs, except epinephrine and norepinephrine, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Because the epinephrine and norepinephrine structure are similar to dopamine; however, the presence of 2-(methylamino) ethyl of epinephrine and 2-aminohydroxy ethyl groups of norepinephrine are caused steric hindrance, which retards the adsorption on the surface of CDs. Hence, the quenching efficiency of epinephrine and norepinephrine are lower than dopamine. The concentration of epinephrine (less than 300 pg mL−1) and norepinephrine (approx. 400 pg mL−1) is much lower in the blood serum than the dopamine, so negligible interference quenching in fluorescence intensity of CDs [2–4]. These results show the specificity and selectivity for this probe toward dopamine detection.

The synthesized CDs can be used for the visual detection of dopamine in aqueous solution and on TLC paper under UV light (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 6(b). The various concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µM) of dopamine were prepared from the stock solution (100 µM). The equal amounts of these solutions were added into CDs solution and stirring for 15 mins. One drop of the mixtures solution was drop cast on TLC paper, and then photographs were taken under UV-light (365 nm). The blue fluorescent colors of CDs are gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of dopamine (0–100 µM) are observed. Similar results are found in the aqueous medium. So, obtained results show that the synthesized CDs can be used as a fluorescence probe for the visual detection of dopamine on TLC paper and aqueous solution.

We have investigated the plausible sensing mechanism behind the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CDs by dopamine. Here could be two possible mechanisms for detecting dopamine by CDs, either inner filter effect (IFE) or FRET [44–54]. In this case, the wide range of absorption spectrum (350–520 nm) of dopamine-quinone should produce a significant spectral overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). This overlapping can say that either FRET or IFE has occurred. Then, fluorescence lifetime decay analysis of CDs is monitored in the presence and absence of dopamine. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is 6.7 ns, and after the addition of dopamine, it decreases to 2.1 ns, as represented in Fig. 7(b). The decrease in the average lifetime value of CDs indicates that the dynamic quenching has occurred [44–48]. These aforementioned observations confirm that quenching occurred through the FRET process and ruled out the possibility of IFE. In the FRET process, the average lifetime of the fluorophore was decreased. However, in the IFE process; the average lifetime is remained constant [44–48]. Simultaneously, the zeta potential analysis was used to determine the surface charge of CDs in the presence and absence of dopamine in the aqueous medium. The zeta potential value of CDs is −2.7 mV after addition of dopamine, it decreased to −35.6 mV, as shown in Fig. 7(c) & (d). The obtained results can say that dopamine was combined with CDs via electrostatic interaction and intermolecular H-bonding.

Dopamine molecules can oxidize into dopamine-quinone and attached on the surface of CDs and form a CDs-dopamine-quinone complex, as represented in Scheme 2
(a). The FRET process occurs when the excited-state electrons of the donor (CDs) return to the ground-state and transfer the energy to the ground-state of the acceptor (dopamine-quinone) goes to the excited state, as Scheme 2 (b-1). Instead, the excited state electrons of CDs can transfer into the LUMO of dopamine-quinone and collide with it. Then, electrons return to the ground state of CDs with radiation of energy, and hence quenched the fluorescence spectra of CDs [49–51], as shown in Scheme 2 (b-2).

The FRET quenching efficiency was calculated by the equation Q = 1-FD-A/FD, [52–54] where FD and FD-A are the emission intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of dopamine, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). The FRET efficiency gradually increased with increasing dopamine concentration and reached 83% when dopamine concentration is 100 μM. The Fo¨rster distance (R0) is calculated by the following formula R0 6 = EFRET × (R0 6 + R6) where R0 is the Fo¨rster distance at which 50% energy transfer occurs between acceptor and donor, R is the distance between donor and acceptor, and EFRET is the FRET efficiency [52–58]. The Fo¨rster distance is calculated and found to be 23.8 Å. This value lies within the 50 Å range, which is ideal for the FRET process. Dopamine provides superior quenching efficiency up to 50 A° Fo¨rster distance and acts as an excellent electron acceptor [11,17,18]. Simultaneously, the FRET efficiency is calculated with respect to time (0–300 mins), the FRET efficiency gradually increases with increasing the time (50 mins) and then constant up to 300 mins, as shown in Fig. 8(b).

CDs are useful fluorescence probes to establish the unknown amount of dopamine in human blood serum and urine samples. We spiked the different concentrations (20, 30 & 40 nM) of dopamine into the human blood serum and urine samples by the standard addition technique. The emission intensities of CDs regularly decrease with increasing dopamine concentration, as shown in Fig. 9 (a) & (b). The results shown in Table 2 indicate that the recovery of dopamine by standard addition technique is 101–108%, demonstrating the current work for dopamine sensing is comparable, accurate, and acceptable for analysis of the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell imaging and cytotoxicity study
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the application of CDs for fluorescence cell imaging of MDA-MB 468 cells are shown in Fig. 10 . The MDA-MB 468 cells were incubated with CDs for 4 h. The cells become brightly illuminated with green and merged fields under excitation of 450 nm (green light). The close observation shows that the CDs spread over the cytoplasm and cell membrane area, but weak fluorescence over the cell nucleus. Hence, the CDs can stain together cell membranes and cytoplasm but not for the cell nucleus. After that, the addition of dopamine solution into the MDA-MB 468 cells and incubated for 1 h. Afterward, the MDA-MB 468 cells were washed with PBS, and the fluorescence microscopy executed imaging experiments; the fluorescent color of MDA-MB 468 cells are diminished. So, the synthesized CDs are useful for the in vitro detection of dopamine. Furthermore, the toxicity of CDs towards MDA-MB 468 cells was measured by MTT assay. The cell viability is around 99% at the concentration of CDs 10 µg/mL. When the concentration was increased from 10 to 100 µg/mL, the cell viability over 87% is observed after incubation of 24 h, as shown in Fig. 9(c). Thus, the results show that the synthesized CDs are non-toxic and possess excellent biocompatibility.

This work has established a label-free fluorescence turn-off strategy for sensitive and selective dopamine detection with the limit of detection 20 nM. The emission intensity of CDs has been successfully quenched by dopamine. The aqueous solution of CDs emits intense blue color under UV-light with a quantum yield of 21%. The probable sensing mechanism has been proposed by the FRET process and supported by the UV–visible spectroscopy, lifetime decay, and zeta potential analysis. All the characterization results more or less confirm the FRET mechanism from different aspects. The synthesized CDs possess outstanding water solubility, good biocompatibility, excellent chemical & photochemical properties, and inertness to interfere with various biomolecules. We have first-time report CDs synthesized from lipase and are directly used to detect dopamine in real samples and effectively used for in vitro sensing of dopamine into MDA-MB 468 cells. This report will open a new avenue for the fabrication of high-performance sensing applications.

Amit Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Archana Kumari: Writing - review & editing. Poulami Mukherjee: Writing - review & editing. Tinku Saikia: Methodology, Investigation, Resources. Kunal Pal: . Sumanta Kumar Sahu: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105590.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
